topic_summary: Backpropagation is the algorithm that makes neural networks learn by efficiently computing how each weight contributed to the error.

loss_function.label: Loss Function
loss_function.description: A measure of how wrong the network's prediction is — the problem that motivates everything.
common_misconceptions: The loss function IS the learning — minimizing it is the same as backprop
Any differentiable function can be a loss function without consequences
Loss of 0 means the model is perfect

gradient.label: Gradient
gradient.description: The direction and magnitude of steepest increase of the loss — tells us how to adjust weights.
common_misconceptions: The gradient points toward the minimum (it actually points toward the maximum)
Gradient is just a derivative — same thing for scalar functions
A zero gradient always means we found the global minimum

chain_rule.label: Chain Rule
chain_rule.description: The calculus rule that lets us compute derivatives through composed functions — the mathematical heart of backprop.
common_misconceptions: Chain rule only applies to two composed functions, not deeper chains
You can apply chain rule in any order
Chain rule and product rule are the same thing

forward_pass.label: Forward Pass
forward_pass.description: The left-to-right computation through the network that produces a prediction.
common_misconceptions: Forward pass includes weight updates
Each layer is independent during the forward pass
The forward pass computes gradients

computational_graph.label: Computational Graph
computational_graph.description: A directed graph representing the sequence of operations — the structure backprop traverses in reverse.
common_misconceptions: The computational graph is fixed — it doesn't change with different inputs
Backprop modifies the computational graph
Each node in the graph is a neuron

backpropagation.label: Backpropagation
backpropagation.description: Efficient reverse-mode automatic differentiation through the computational graph using the chain rule.
common_misconceptions: Backprop IS gradient descent — they are the same algorithm
Backprop updates the weights directly
Backprop just computes derivatives of each weight independently

vanishing_gradient.label: Vanishing Gradient
vanishing_gradient.description: The problem where gradients shrink exponentially in deep networks, making early layers learn very slowly.
common_misconceptions: Vanishing gradient only happens with sigmoid activations
Deeper networks always have vanishing gradients
Batch normalization completely solves vanishing gradients
